content.problemStatement:
Zonal Computing Olympiad 2015, 29 Nov 2014
 An interval is a pair of positive integers [a, b] with a ≤ b. It is meant to denote the set of integers that lie between the values a and b. For example [3,5] denotes the set {3,4,5} while the interval [3, 3] denotes the set {3}.



We say that an interval [a, b] is covered by an integer i, if i belongs to the set defined by [a, b]. For example interval [3, 5] is covered by 3 and so is the interval [3, 3].


Given a set of intervals I, and a set of integers S we say that I is covered by S if for each interval [a, b] in I there is an integer i in S such that [a, b] is covered by i. For example, the set {[3, 5], [3, 3]} is covered by the set {3}. The set of intervals {[6, 9], [3, 5], [4, 8]} is covered by the set {4, 5, 8}. It is also covered by the set {4, 7}.


We would like to compute, for any set of intervals I, the size of the smallest set S that covers it. You can check that for the set of intervals {[6, 9], [3, 5], [4, 8]} the answer is 2 while for the set of intervals {[3, 5], [3, 3]} the answer is 1.


Your program should take the set of intervals as input and output the size of the smallest set that covers it as the answer.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N, the number of intervals. The next N lines each contain two integers a and b (a ≤ b) describing an interval [a, b].
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the size of the smallest set of integers that covers all intervals of that test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There is only one test case. The two intervals [3,5] and [3,3] both contain the integer 3, so a single point {3} covers them.
One optimal covering set is {4,8}. Point 4 covers intervals [3,5] and [4,8]; point 8 covers [6,9] and also [4,8]. Hence the minimum size is 2.
First test case: all three intervals share the integer 7, so answer is 1.\nSecond test case: intervals can be covered with points 2 and 4, giving answer 2.

solution.approach: For each test case we sort the intervals by their right endpoint (b) in non‑decreasing order. We then iterate through the sorted list, keeping the last chosen point (initially the end of the first interval). If the current interval starts after this point, we must choose a new point – we place it at the current interval's right endpoint because this choice leaves the maximum room for covering subsequent intervals. The number of chosen points is the answer. This classic greedy algorithm is optimal for the interval covering problem.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting, plus O(N) for the scan.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the intervals of a test case.
